skill.name: cirq
skill.description: Guidance for using Cirq to build, simulate, optimize, and run quantum circuits with Python.
skill.summary: Build and analyze Cirq quantum circuits, simulations, transformations, noise models, and hardware workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the alerts are overwhelmingly Markdown examples and quantum-computing terminology, not executable skill logic. The remaining low-risk concerns are documentation for package installation, cloud provider URLs, and placeholder API-token setup for quantum hardware services.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to fenced Markdown examples for installing Cirq packages and running documented setup commands. They are not executable code inside the skill, but users should review commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are visible Markdown code fences and documented examples. No autonomous command runner or hidden execution path was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Hardware Credentials in Documentation
low_findings.description: The skill documents API keys and tokens for IonQ, AQT, and Pasqal using placeholder values. This is legitimate setup guidance, but users should avoid pasting real secrets into prompts or shared logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are placeholders such as your_api_key and your_token, not embedded credentials. The context is provider authentication guidance for quantum hardware services.

low_findings.title: Provider URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs refer to official documentation and quantum hardware provider endpoints. They do not send data by themselves, and no exfiltration logic was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in setup examples and resource lists. There is no code path that collects secrets or posts user data to these endpoints.

low_findings.title: Quantum Terms Misclassified as Cryptographic or C2 Indicators
low_findings.description: Static weak-crypto and C2 alerts match quantum terms, serialization text, or gate names such as CNOT and state-vector examples. These are domain concepts, not malicious control logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are quantum circuit and probability examples. They do not implement cryptography, beaconing, command channels, or malware behavior.

low_findings.title: Azure Resource Identifier Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal alert is a placeholder Azure Quantum resource identifier using ellipses. It is not filesystem access and does not read or write local paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside an Azure workspace configuration example. It represents a cloud resource path, not user-controlled filesystem traversal.

content.value_statement: Quantum developers need precise guidance for circuits, simulation, noise, and hardware workflows. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused Cirq references for practical quantum programming tasks.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to construct Cirq circuits with qubits, gates, measurements, and observables.
Guides exact, noisy, and parameter-sweep simulations with Cirq simulators.
Describes circuit transformation, decomposition, optimization, and gateset targeting workflows.
Summarizes hardware integration patterns for Google Quantum AI, IonQ, AQT, Pasqal, and Azure Quantum.
Covers experiment patterns for VQE, QAOA, QPE, benchmarking, and result visualization.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not live access to quantum hardware.
Users must install Cirq and provider packages in their own environment.
Hardware runs require valid provider accounts, credentials, quotas, and possible costs.
Large simulations can exceed local memory or runtime limits.

use_cases.title: Prototype Quantum Algorithms
use_cases.description: Design and test Cirq circuits for algorithms such as VQE, QAOA, and phase estimation.

use_cases.title: Compare Simulation Strategies
use_cases.description: Choose state-vector, density-matrix, noisy, or sweep-based simulation patterns for a circuit.

use_cases.title: Prepare Hardware Experiments
use_cases.description: Review provider setup, device validation, qubit selection, and hardware optimization steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Circuit
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me build a simple Cirq circuit with two qubits, entanglement, measurement, and a short explanation of each operation.

prompt_templates.title: Simulate Parameter Sweeps
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to run a Cirq parameter sweep for a rotation angle and summarize the measurement results clearly.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize for Hardware
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Cirq circuit design and recommend transformations that reduce depth and match a target hardware gateset.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Quantum Experiment
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a reproducible Cirq experiment for a variational algorithm, including data collection, analysis, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: User asks how to start a two-qubit Cirq simulation.
output_examples.output: A short circuit plan with qubit selection, entangling operations, measurement keys, simulator choice, and expected histogram interpretation.

output_examples.input: User asks why noisy simulation is slow.
output_examples.output: A diagnosis explaining density-matrix cost, repetition tradeoffs, noise placement, and options for reducing qubit count or model complexity.

output_examples.input: User asks how to prepare for a hardware run.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering provider package setup, credential handling, device validation, topology constraints, calibration review, and simulator testing first.

best_practices: Validate circuits on simulators before using quantum hardware providers.
Use symbolic parameters and sweeps for repeatable experiments.
Keep credentials in local environment variables or secret stores, not prompts.
anti_patterns: Do not run hardware jobs before checking device constraints and cost implications.
Do not paste real API keys into shared prompts, notebooks, or logs.
Do not use density-matrix simulation for large pure-state circuits unless noise modeling requires it.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants answer Cirq questions about circuits, simulation, noise, transformations, experiments, and hardware workflows.

faq.question: Does it execute quantum programs?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Execution happens only in the user environment.

faq.question: Which providers are covered?
faq.answer: The references mention Google Quantum AI, IonQ, Azure Quantum, AQT, and Pasqal setup patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with noisy simulation?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers noise channels, density-matrix simulation, readout noise, characterization, and mitigation concepts.

faq.question: Is it safe for credentials?
faq.answer: The skill uses placeholders, but users should keep real provider keys outside prompts and shared files.

faq.question: Can it replace official Cirq documentation?
faq.answer: No. It is a focused assistant reference. Users should verify provider APIs and account requirements with official documentation.
